title: The Adjoint of a Linear Operator

content.title: Existence of the Adjoint
content.content:
Let $V$ be a finite-dimensional inner product space and $T: V \to V$ 
a linear operator. There exists a unique linear operator $T^*: V \to V$ 
such that
\[
\langle Tv, w \rangle = \langle v, T^*w \rangle \quad \text{for all } v, w \in V
\]
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
For fixed $w$, the map $v \mapsto \langle Tv, w \rangle$ is a linear 
functional on $V$. By the Riesz Representation Theorem, there exists 
a unique $u \in V$ with $\langle Tv, w \rangle = \langle v, u \rangle$ 
for all $v$. Define $T^*(w) = u$. Linearity of $T^*$ follows from 
uniqueness in the Riesz theorem.

content.title: Adjoint
content.content: The operator $T^*$ is called the **adjoint** of $T$.

content.title: Properties of the Adjoint
content.content:
Let $S, T$ be operators on $V$ and $\alpha \in \FF$.

1. $(T^*)^* = T$

2. $(S + T)^* = S^* + T^*$

3. $(\alpha T)^* = \conj{\alpha} T^*$

4. $(ST)^* = T^* S^*$

5. $I^* = I$

6. $\ker T^* = (\im T)^\perp$

7. $\im T^* = (\ker T)^\perp$
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Matrix of Adjoint
content.content: If $\mathcal{B}$ is an orthonormal basis, then $[T^*]_{\mathcal{B}} = ([T]_{\mathcal{B}})^*$.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Self-Adjoint
content.content:
An operator $T$ is **self-adjoint** (or 
**Hermitian**) if $T = T^*$.

For real inner product spaces, self-adjoint is called **symmetric**.

content.title: Real Eigenvalues
content.content: All eigenvalues of a self-adjoint operator are real.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $Tv = \lambda v$ with $v \neq 0$. Then
\[
\lambda \|v\|^2 = \lambda \langle v, v \rangle = \langle \lambda v, v \rangle = \langle Tv, v \rangle = \langle v, T^*v \rangle = \langle v, Tv \rangle = \langle v, \lambda v \rangle = \conj{\lambda} \|v\|^2
\]
Since $\|v\|^2 \neq 0$, we have $\lambda = \conj{\lambda}$, so $\lambda \in \RR$.

content.title: Orthogonal Eigenspaces
content.content:
Eigenvectors of a self-adjoint operator corresponding to distinct 
eigenvalues are orthogonal.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
Let $Tv = \lambda v$ and $Tw = \mu w$ with $\lambda \neq \mu$. Then
\[
\lambda \langle v, w \rangle = \langle \lambda v, w \rangle = \langle Tv, w \rangle = \langle v, Tw \rangle = \langle v, \mu w \rangle = \mu \langle v, w \rangle
\]
since $\mu \in \RR$. Thus $(\lambda - \mu)\langle v, w \rangle = 0$, 
and since $\lambda \neq \mu$, we have $\langle v, w \rangle = 0$.

content.title: Spectral Theorem (Self-Adjoint)
content.content:
Let $T$ be a self-adjoint operator on a finite-dimensional inner 
product space $V$. Then there exists an orthonormal basis of $V$ 
consisting of eigenvectors of $T$.

Equivalently, $T$ is orthogonally diagonalizable.
content.label: Theorem

content.content:
By induction on $\dim V$. The case $\dim V = 1$ is trivial.

For the inductive step: $T$ has an eigenvalue $\lambda$ (real by the 
previous theorem). Let $v$ be a unit eigenvector. Set $W = \spn\{v\}^\perp$.

Claim: $W$ is $T$-invariant. For $w \in W$:
\[
\langle Tw, v \rangle = \langle w, Tv \rangle = \langle w, \lambda v \rangle = \lambda \langle w, v \rangle = 0
\]
so $Tw \in W$.

The restriction $T|_W$ is self-adjoint on $W$. By induction, $W$ has 
an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors. Together with $v$, this gives 
an orthonormal eigenvector basis for $V$.

content.title: Normal Operators
content.problem:
1. Self-adjoint: $T = T^*$, so $TT^* = T^2 = T^*T$

2. Unitary: $T^*T = TT^* = I$

3. Skew-adjoint: $T^* = -T$, so $TT^* = -T^2 = T^*T$

content.title: Properties of Normal Operators
content.content:
Let $T$ be normal. Then:

1. $\|Tv\| = \|T^*v\|$ for all $v$

2. $\ker T = \ker T^*$

3. If $Tv = \lambda v$, then $T^*v = \conj{\lambda}v$

4. Eigenvectors for distinct eigenvalues are orthogonal
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Spectral Theorem (Normal, Complex)
content.content:
Let $T$ be a normal operator on a finite-dimensional complex inner 
product space. Then $T$ is unitarily diagonalizable: there exists 
an orthonormal basis of eigenvectors.
content.label: Theorem

content.title: Positive Operator
content.content:
A self-adjoint operator $T$ is **positive** 
(or positive semidefinite) if $\langle Tv, v \rangle \geq 0$ for all $v$.

It is **positive definite** if $\langle Tv, v \rangle > 0$ for all $v \neq 0$.

content.title: Characterization of Positive Operators
content.content:
For a self-adjoint operator $T$, the following are equivalent:

1. $T$ is positive

2. All eigenvalues of $T$ are nonnegative

3. $T = S^*S$ for some operator $S$

4. $T = R^2$ for some positive self-adjoint $R$
content.label: Theorem
